---
title: "IC"
---

Model averaging of lattice determinations using custom Information Criteria (e.g. Akaike Information Criterion, AIC).

Each criterion is based on the values of the \chi^2 residual, number of parameters and number of data points.

Conventions here:
    - "AIC"  :=  chi2 + 2 * n_par - 2 * n_data,
    - "AICc" :=  chi2 + 2*npar*(1.0+npar)/real(ndata-npar-1),
    - "BIC"  :=  chi2 + npar*log(real(ndata)),
    - "AIC_Ncut" := chi2 + 2*npar + 2*(Nmax - ndata),
    - "AIC_BMW"  := chi2 + 2*n_par + (Nmax - n_data)

Main reference: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2208.14983. See Eqs. (65),(64),(62)
    Used in: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.08852, eq. 17
    ACHTUNG: Typo in https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.12347, eq. 161: factor 2 in front of n_data is missing

.. ldt-id:: IC-module

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Source:** [`model_averaging/IC.py`](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py)
:::


## API Reference

#### `get_IC` {#get_ic}

```python
def get_IC(ch2: np.ndarray, n_par: np.ndarray, n_data: np.ndarray, IC: valid_IC, Nmax: Optional[int]=None) -> np.ndarray
```

Compute an information criterion `IC` for each model.
NOTE: This function whould run on a single sample (e.g. bootstrap or jackknife)

Main reference: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2208.14983. See Eqs. (65),(64),(62)

NOTE: 
    The difference between AIC and AIC_BMW lies in the missing factor of 2 in front of n_data for the BMW's one:
    - AIC:      https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.08852, eq. 17
    - AIC_BMW:  https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.12347, eq. 161 (BMW lattice collaboration)

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L30){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `get_weights` {#get_weights}

```python
def get_weights(ch2: np.ndarray, n_par: np.ndarray, n_data: np.ndarray, IC: valid_IC, Nmax: Optional[int]=None) -> np.ndarray
```

Returns the (unnormalized) 1d array of weights as in eq. 17 of https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.08852
The inputs are the array of models (each with a \chi^2) the number of fit parameters and number of points

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L69){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `get_Pi` {#get_pi}

```python
def get_Pi(y: np.ndarray, m: np.ndarray, sigma: np.ndarray, lam: np.float64)
```

Returns the Cumulative Density Functions (CDF) P_i(y, lambda) of Eq. 162 of https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.12347
for all the values of the array "y".

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L79){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `get_P` {#get_p}

```python
def get_P(y: np.ndarray, w: np.ndarray, m: np.ndarray, sigma: np.ndarray, lam: np.float64)
```

Returns the sum of the Cumulative Density Functions (CDF) P_i(y, lambda) of Eq. 162 of https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.12347
for all the values of the array "y". These values are the domain of values that we can get from each model, 
i.e. the m[i] should fall into the interval of the y values.

Remark: We numerically evaluate the CDF at some values of y (specified by the array), 
and estimate the percentiles from those. Thus, the resolution in the y[i] should be much larger that the sigma[i]

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L94){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `get_P_from_bootstraps` {#get_p_from_bootstraps}

```python
def get_P_from_bootstraps(y: BootstrapSamples, w: np.ndarray, lam: np.float64)
```

CDFs from boostrap samples 

We build the CDF numerically, by counting how many occurrencies of y we have before each y_0.
This is done as follows:

Step 0 (optional): we rescale the variance of the bootstrap samples
Step 1: We consider all the y values, i.e. from each bootstrap and each each model, all together
Step 2: We build the CDFs of each model, scaled by its weight (normalized to 1).
        This is done by building, for each model k, a fictitious histogram count (with binning 1). 
        Only for the values of "y" of the k-th model, we set the count to the weight w_k.
Step 3: The cumulative sums of these fictitious histogram gives the CDF 
        in terms of the whole array of values coming from all the models.

Arguments:
    y: array of size (N_bts, n_models)
    lam: scaling parameter for the variance --> sqrt(lambda) for the uncertainty
    
Returns:
    dictionary with the results of this procedure

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L110){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `get_y16_y50_y84` {#get_y16_y50_y84}

```python
def get_y16_y50_y84(w: np.ndarray, m: np.ndarray, sigma: np.ndarray, lam: np.float64, ymin: np.float64, ymax: np.float64, eps: np.float64)
```

Returns percentile values y16, y50 (the median) and y84

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L146){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `get_mean_and_sigma2` {#get_mean_and_sigma2}

```python
def get_mean_and_sigma2(y16, y50, y84)
```

Returns mean and variance from the percentiles (assuming the distribution is Gaussian)

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L156){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `get_sigma2_contributions` {#get_sigma2_contributions}

```python
def get_sigma2_contributions(w: np.ndarray, m: np.ndarray, sigma: np.ndarray, ymin: np.float64, ymax: np.float64, eps: np.float64, lambda1=1.0, lambda2=2.0)
```

Statistical and systematic variances using the AIC model averaging

w: weights
m: means of the models
sigma: statistical uncertainties of the models
ymin, ymax, eps: parameters defining the interval for numerically reproducing the CDF finely enough

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L165){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

### `with_CDF` {#with_cdf}

```python
class with_CDF
```

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L190){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `with_CDF.get_rescaled_y` {#with_cdf-get_rescaled_y}

```python
def get_rescaled_y(y: np.ndarray, P: np.ndarray, lam: float)
```

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L192){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `with_CDF.get_P` {#with_cdf-get_p}

```python
def get_P(y: List[np.ndarray], w: np.ndarray)
```

CDFs from list of models

We build the CDF numerically, by counting how many occurrencies of y we have before each y_0.
This is done as follows:

Step 1: We consider all the y values
Step 2: We build the CDFs of each model, scaled by its weight (normalized to 1).
        This is done by building, for each model k, a fictitious histogram count (with binning 1). 
        Only for the values of "y" of the k-th model, we set the count to the weight w_k.
Step 3: The cumulative sums of these fictitious histogram gives the CDF 
        in terms of the whole array of values coming from all the models.

Arguments:
    y: list of size (n_models). Each item is an array of values of "y", whose distribution determine their CDF. The array can have different sizes.
    w: array of length n_models
    
Returns:
    dictionary with the results of this procedure

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L198){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `with_CDF.get_quantiles` {#with_cdf-get_quantiles}

```python
def get_quantiles(y: np.ndarray, P: np.ndarray, quantiles=[16, 50, 84])
```

Dictionary of quantiles corresponding to 
16%, 50%, 84% of probabilities

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L229){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---

#### `with_CDF.get_contributions` {#with_cdf-get_contributions}

```python
def get_contributions(y1: np.ndarray, y2: np.ndarray, P1: np.ndarray, P2: np.ndarray, lam1: float, lam2: float)
```

Contributions to the total error.

!!! ACHTUNG !!!
The result may be inconsistent if one chooses the wrong values of lam1 and lam2.
In fact, the procedure works if (lam2*sigma^2_tot_l1 - lam1*sigma2_tot_l2) > 0. 
The user is expected to use the results of this function to update the value of lam2 in order to get all positive variances.

These are obtained by using 2 different AICs, where each time we rescaled the statistical uncertainty with lam1 and lam2 respectively.
NOTE: by "statistical" we mean the uncertainty of the individual models before the AIC. The latter can be the total error coming from a sub-model averaging

This function returns:
  - mean: 50% quantile of the total distribution
  - sigma^2_stat: variance due to the statistical fluctuations
  - sigma^2 syst: variance due to the systematics

[View source ↗](https://github.com/simone-romiti/lattice_data_tools/blob/main/model_averaging/IC.py#L246){.source-link style='font-size:0.8em;'}

---
